title: Hands-on Exercise 3 (Part 2)
text:
When telling a visually-driven data story, animated graphics tends to attract the interest of the audience and make deeper impression than static graphics. In this hands-on exercise, you will learn how to create animated data visualisation by using gganimate and plotly r packages. At the same time, you will also learn how to (i) reshape data by using tidyr package, and (ii) process, wrangle and transform data by using dplyr package.


When creating animations, the plot does not actually move. Instead, many individual plots are built and then stitched together as movie frames, just like an old-school flip book or cartoon. Each frame is a different plot when conveying motion, which is built using some relevant subset of the aggregate data. The subset drives the flow of the animation when stitched back together.



Before we dive into the steps for creating an animated statistical graph, it’s important to understand some of the key concepts and terminology related to this type of visualization.

Frame: In an animated line graph, each frame represents a different point in time or a different category. When the frame changes, the data points on the graph are updated to reflect the new data.
Animation Attributes: The animation attributes are the settings that control how the animation behaves. For example, you can specify the duration of each frame, the easing function used to transition between frames, and whether to start the animation from the current frame or from the beginning.








pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)




Write a code chunk to import Data worksheet from GlobalPopulation.xls Excel workbook by using appropriate R package from tidyverse family.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("../data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%
  mutate_each_(funs(factor(.)), col) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

Unfortunately, mutate_each_() was deprecated in dplyr 0.7.0 and funs() was deprecated in dplyr 0.8.0. In view of this, we will re-write the code by using mutate_at() as shown in the code chunk below.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("../data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%
  mutate_at(col, as.factor) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

Instead of using mutate_at(), across() can be used to derive the same outputs.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("../data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%
  mutate(across(col, as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))





gganimate extends the grammar of graphics as implemented by ggplot2 to include the description of animation. It does this by providing a range of new grammar classes that can be added to the plot object in order to customise how it should change with time.

transition_*() defines how the data should be spread out and how it relates to itself across time.
view_*() defines how the positional scales should change along the animation.
shadow_*() defines how data from other points in time should be presented in the given point in time.
enter_*()/exit_*() defines how new data should appear and how old data should disappear during the course of the animation.
ease_aes() defines how different aesthetics should be eased during transitions.



In the code chunk below, the basic ggplot2 functions are used to create a static bubble plot.

ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') 











In the code chunk below,

transition_time() of gganimate is used to create transition through distinct states in time (i.e. Year).
ease_aes() is used to control easing of aesthetics. The default is linear. Other methods are: quadratic, cubic, quartic, quintic, sine, circular, exponential, elastic, back, and bounce.


ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') +
  transition_time(Year) +       
  ease_aes('linear')          














In this sub-section, you will learn how to create an animated bubble plot by using ggplotly() method.

gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
       aes(x = Old, 
           y = Young, 
           size = Population, 
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young')

ggplotly(gg)





Notice that although show.legend = FALSE argument was used, the legend still appears on the plot. To overcome this problem, theme(legend.position='none') should be used as shown in the plot and code chunk below.

gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
       aes(x = Old, 
           y = Young, 
           size = Population, 
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') + 
  theme(legend.position='none')

ggplotly(gg)








In this sub-section, you will learn how to create an animated bubble plot by using plot_ly() method.

bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x = ~Old, 
          y = ~Young, 
          size = ~Population, 
          color = ~Continent,
          sizes = c(2, 100),
          frame = ~Year, 
          text = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type = 'scatter',
          mode = 'markers'
          ) %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)
bp

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 (Part 2)
text:
When telling a visually-driven data story, animated graphics tends to attract the interest of the audience and make deeper impression than static graphics. In this hands-on exercise, you will learn how to create animated data visualisation by using gganimate and plotly r packages. At the same time, you will also learn how to (i) reshape data by using tidyr package, and (ii) process, wrangle and transform data by using dplyr package.


When creating animations, the plot does not actually move. Instead, many individual plots are built and then stitched together as movie frames, just like an old-school flip book or cartoon. Each frame is a different plot when conveying motion, which is built using some relevant subset of the aggregate data. The subset drives the flow of the animation when stitched back together.



Before we dive into the steps for creating an animated statistical graph, it’s important to understand some of the key concepts and terminology related to this type of visualization.

Frame: In an animated line graph, each frame represents a different point in time or a different category. When the frame changes, the data points on the graph are updated to reflect the new data.
Animation Attributes: The animation attributes are the settings that control how the animation behaves. For example, you can specify the duration of each frame, the easing function used to transition between frames, and whether to start the animation from the current frame or from the beginning.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 (Part 1)
text:
In this hands-on exercise, you will learn how to create interactive data visualisation by using functions provided by ggiraph and plotly packages.



The code chunk below will be used to accomplish the task.

pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, DT, tidyverse) 


The downloaded binary packages are in
    /var/folders/ry/c12ynh2n3lvc78fj89rgdbtw0000gn/T//RtmpTZrf9O/downloaded_packages



The downloaded binary packages are in
    /var/folders/ry/c12ynh2n3lvc78fj89rgdbtw0000gn/T//RtmpTZrf9O/downloaded_packages



The downloaded binary packages are in
    /var/folders/ry/c12ynh2n3lvc78fj89rgdbtw0000gn/T//RtmpTZrf9O/downloaded_packages





In this section, Exam_data.csv provided will be used. Using read_csv() of readr package, import Exam_data.csv into R.
The code chunk below uses read_csv() of readr package to import Exam_data.csv data file into R and save it as a tibble data frame called exam_data.

exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")






Below shows a typical code chunk to plot an interactive statistical graph by using ggiraph package. Notice that the code chunk consists of two parts. First, a ggplot object will be created. Next, girafe() of ggiraph will be used to create an interactive svg object.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)





Notice that two steps are involved. First, an interactive version of ggplot2 geom (i.e. geom_dotplot_interactive()) will be used to create the basic graph. Then, girafe() will be used to generate an svg object to be displayed on an html page.
By hovering the mouse pointer on a data point of interest, the student’s ID will be displayed.



The content of the tooltip can be customised by including a list object as shown in the code chunk below.

exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS)) 

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)





The first three lines of codes in the code chunk create a new field called tooltip. At the same time, it populates text in ID and CLASS fields into the newly created field. Next, this newly created field is used as tooltip field as shown in the code of line 7.
By hovering the mouse pointer on a data point of interest, the student’s ID and Class will be displayed.



Code chunk below uses opts_tooltip() of ggiraph to customize tooltip rendering by add css declarations.

tooltip_css <- "background-color:white;
font-style:bold; color:black;"

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(
      css = tooltip_css))
)





Notice that the background colour of the tooltip is black and the font colour is white and bold.



Code chunk below shows an advanced way to customise tooltip. In this example, a function is used to compute 90% confident interval of the mean. The derived statistics are then displayed in the tooltip.

tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE),
) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip = after_stat(  
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, size = 0.2
  )

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)








Code chunk below shows the second interactive feature of ggiraph, namely data_id.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(           
    aes(data_id = CLASS),             
    stackgroups = TRUE,               
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618                      
)                                        





Interactivity: Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over.
Note that the default value of the hover css is hover_css = “fill:orange;”.



In the code chunk below, css codes are used to change the highlighting effect.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)                                        





Interactivity: Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over.
Note: Different from the previous example, in this example the CSS customisation requests are encoded directly.



There are times that we want to combine tooltip and hover effect on the interactive statistical graph as shown in the code chunk below.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)                                        





Interactivity: Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over. At the same time, the tooltip will show the CLASS.



onclick argument of ggiraph provides hotlink interactivity on the web.
The code chunk below shows an example of onclick.

exam_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
"https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618)                                        





Interactivity: Web document link with a data object will be displayed on the web browser upon mouse click.



Coordinated multiple views methods has been implemented in the data visualisation below

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(code = print(p1 + p2), 
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 3,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
         )
       ) 





The data_id aesthetic is critical to link observations between plots and the tooltip aesthetic is optional but nice to have when mouse over a point.




Plotly’s R graphing library create interactive web graphics from ggplot2 graphs and/or a custom interface to the (MIT-licensed) JavaScript library plotly.js inspired by the grammar of graphics. Different from other plotly platform, plot.R is free and open source.
There are two ways to create interactive graph by using plotly, they are:

by using plot_ly(), and
by using ggplotly()



The tabset below shows an example a basic interactive plot created by using plot_ly().

plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
             x = ~MATHS, 
             y = ~ENGLISH)








In the code chunk below, color argument is mapped to a qualitative visual variable (i.e. RACE).

plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~ENGLISH, 
        y = ~MATHS, 
        color = ~RACE)








The code chunk below plots an interactive scatter plot by using ggplotly().

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)








The creation of a coordinated linked plot by using plotly involves three steps:

highlight_key() of plotly package is used as shared data.
Two scatterplots will be created by using ggplot2 functions.
Lastly, subplot() of plotly package is used to place them next to each other side-by-side.


d <- highlight_key(exam_data)
p1 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

p2 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
subplot(ggplotly(p1),
        ggplotly(p2))





Thing to learn from the code chunk:
highlight_key() simply creates an object of class crosstalk::SharedData. Visit this link to learn more about crosstalk.




Crosstalk is an add-on to the htmlwidgets package. It extends htmlwidgets with a set of classes, functions, and conventions for implementing cross-widget interactions (currently, linked brushing and filtering).


A wrapper of the JavaScript Library DataTables
Data objects in R can be rendered as HTML tables using the JavaScript library ‘DataTables’ (typically via R Markdown or Shiny).

DT::datatable(exam_data, class= "compact")









d <- highlight_key(exam_data) 
p <- ggplot(d, 
            aes(ENGLISH, 
                MATHS)) + 
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p),        
                "plotly_selected")  

crosstalk::bscols(gg,               
                  DT::datatable(d), 
                  widths = 5)        















Things to learn from the code chunk:

highlight() is a function of plotly package. It sets a variety of options for brushing (i.e., highlighting) multiple plots. These options are primarily designed for linking multiple plotly graphs, and may not behave as expected when linking plotly to another htmlwidget package via crosstalk. In some cases, other htmlwidgets will respect these options, such as persistent selection in leaflet.
bscols() is a helper function of crosstalk package. It makes it easy to put HTML elements side by side. It can be called directly from the console but is especially designed to work in an R Markdown document. Warning: This will bring in all of Bootstrap!.

)





Notice that the background colour of the tooltip is black and the font colour is white and bold.



Code chunk below shows an advanced way to customise tooltip. In this example, a function is used to compute 90% confident interval of the mean. The derived statistics are then displayed in the tooltip.

tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE),
) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip = after_stat(  
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, size = 0.2
  )

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)








Code chunk below shows the second interactive feature of ggiraph, namely data_id.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(           
    aes(data_id = CLASS),             
    stackgroups = TRUE,               
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618                      
)                                        





Interactivity: Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over.
Note that the default value of the hover css is hover_css = “fill:orange;”.



In the code chunk below, css codes are used to change the highlighting effect.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)                                        





Interactivity: Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over.
Note: Different from the previous example, in this example the CSS customisation requests are encoded directly.



There are times that we want to combine tooltip and hover effect on the interactive statistical graph as shown in the code chunk below.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)                                        





Interactivity: Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over. At the same time, the tooltip will show the CLASS.



onclick argument of ggiraph provides hotlink interactivity on the web.
The code chunk below shows an example of onclick.

exam_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
"https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618)                                        





Interactivity: Web document link with a data object will be displayed on the web browser upon mouse click.



Coordinated multiple views methods has been implemented in the data visualisation below

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(code = print(p1 + p2), 
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 3,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
         )
       ) 





The data_id aesthetic is critical to link observations between plots and the tooltip aesthetic is optional but nice to have when mouse over a point.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 (Part 1)
text:
Plotly’s R graphing library create interactive web graphics from ggplot2 graphs and/or a custom interface to the (MIT-licensed) JavaScript library plotly.js inspired by the grammar of graphics. Different from other plotly platform, plot.R is free and open source.
There are two ways to create interactive graph by using plotly, they are:

by using plot_ly(), and
by using ggplotly()



The tabset below shows an example a basic interactive plot created by using plot_ly().

plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
             x = ~MATHS, 
             y = ~ENGLISH)








In the code chunk below, color argument is mapped to a qualitative visual variable (i.e. RACE).

plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~ENGLISH, 
        y = ~MATHS, 
        color = ~RACE)








The code chunk below plots an interactive scatter plot by using ggplotly().

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)








The creation of a coordinated linked plot by using plotly involves three steps:

highlight_key() of plotly package is used as shared data.
Two scatterplots will be created by using ggplot2 functions.
Lastly, subplot() of plotly package is used to place them next to each other side-by-side.


d <- highlight_key(exam_data)
p1 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

p2 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
subplot(ggplotly(p1),
        ggplotly(p2))





Thing to learn from the code chunk:
highlight_key() simply creates an object of class crosstalk::SharedData. Visit this link to learn more about crosstalk.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 (Part 1)
text:
Crosstalk is an add-on to the htmlwidgets package. It extends htmlwidgets with a set of classes, functions, and conventions for implementing cross-widget interactions (currently, linked brushing and filtering).


A wrapper of the JavaScript Library DataTables
Data objects in R can be rendered as HTML tables using the JavaScript library ‘DataTables’ (typically via R Markdown or Shiny).

DT::datatable(exam_data, class= "compact")









d <- highlight_key(exam_data) 
p <- ggplot(d, 
            aes(ENGLISH, 
                MATHS)) + 
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p),        
                "plotly_selected")  

crosstalk::bscols(gg,               
                  DT::datatable(d), 
                  widths = 5)        















Things to learn from the code chunk:

highlight() is a function of plotly package. It sets a variety of options for brushing (i.e., highlighting) multiple plots. These options are primarily designed for linking multiple plotly graphs, and may not behave as expected when linking plotly to another htmlwidget package via crosstalk. In some cases, other htmlwidgets will respect these options, such as persistent selection in leaflet.
bscols() is a helper function of crosstalk package. It makes it easy to put HTML elements side by side. It can be called directly from the console but is especially designed to work in an R Markdown document. Warning: This will bring in all of Bootstrap!.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
pacman::p_load(
  ggrepel, patchwork, 
  ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
  tidyverse
)





exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")





One of the challenge in plotting statistical graph is annotation, especially with large number of data points.

ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, size = 0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = 0.5, 
             vjust = -0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")











ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")












ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes, they are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 










ggthemes provides ‘ggplot2’ themes that replicate the look of plots by Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, Fivethirtyeight, The Economist, ‘Stata’, ‘Excel’, and The Wall Street Journal, among others.
In the example below, The Economist theme is used.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()











hrbrthemes package provides a base theme that focuses on typographic elements, including where various labels are placed as well as the fonts that are used.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()








The second goal centers around productivity for a production workflow. In fact, this “production workflow” is the context for where the elements of hrbrthemes should be used.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")












It is not unusual that multiple graphs are required to tell a compelling visual story. There are several ggplot2 extensions provide functions to compose figure with multiple graphs. In this section, you will learn how to create composite plot by combining multiple graphs. First, let us create three statistical graphics by using the code chunk below.

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

Next

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

Lastly, we will draw a scatterplot for English score versus Maths score by as shown below

p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")







p1 + p2












(p1 / p2) | p3












((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')











Beside providing functions to place plots next to each other based on the provided layout. With inset_element() of patchwork, we can place one or several plots or graphic elements freely on top or below another plot.

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)











Figure below is created by combining patchwork and theme_economist() of ggthemes package discussed earlier.

patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()

title: Take-home Exercise 1 - Requirements
text:
The Tasks
The specific tasks of this study are:

Apply suitable univariate visual analytics techniques to explore the distributions and characteristics of numerical and categorical variables.
Apply suitable bivariate visual analytics techniques to investigate and statistically validate factors influencing the profitability and volatility of the insurance portfolio.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 - Requirements
text:
The Designing Tool
The data should be processed by using appropriate tidyverse family of packages and the visual analytics must be performed by using ggplot2 and its extensions.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 - Requirements
text:
Deliverables
The deliverables for this assignment are as follows:

1. Executive Summary
Format: Quarto presentation format (revealjs)
Requirements: - Must contain between 10 and 15 slides, inclusive of the cover page and contents page - Each slide must include Speaker Notes not exceeding 150 words - Speaker notes should provide interpretation and discussion of the patterns, distribution, and statistical insights revealed


2. Reproducible Technical Report
Format: Quarto HTML format
Requirements: The report must:

Document the procedures used to prepare the analytical sandbox
Justify the selection of the visual analytics techniques employed
Describe the implementation and application of the visual analytics methods

title: Take-home Exercise 1 - Requirements
text:
Grading Criteria
This assignment will be graded using the following criteria:

Data Wrangling (20 marks)
This is an important aspect of visual analytics. You will be assessed on your ability to employ appropriate tidyverse functions to import, extract, clean, transform and process data. Besides assessing your ability to use the tidyverse functions, this criterion also includes your ability to clean and derive appropriate variables to meet the visual analysis need.


Visual Analysis (30 marks)
In this exercise, you are expected to utilise the visual analytics methods introduced on lesson slides and readings materials included on the weekly course outlines, along with the R methods learned during the hands-on exercises, to perform your analysis. You will be assessed on your ability to apply these methods correctly and to provide accurate interpretations and discussions of the analysis results.


Visual Communication (20 marks)
In this section, your ability to effectively communicate visual analysis results through user-friendly visual representations will be assessed. Since this course is focused on visual analysis, it is crucial that you demonstrate proficiency in using appropriate visualization techniques, principles and best practices to clearly convey the findings of your analysis.


Reproducibility (15 marks)
This is a key learning outcome of this course. You will be assessed on your ability to thoroughly document the data preparation, visual analytics and visual design procedures.


Bonus (15 marks)
Demonstrate your ability to employ methods beyond what you had learned in class to gain insights from the data. The methods used must be visual analytics in nature.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Contents

Introduction & Objectives
Data Overview
Normality Assessment
Premium Distribution
Claims Distribution
Profitability Patterns
Premium vs Claims Relationship
Profitability by Policy Type
Total Exposure vs Profitability
Profitability by Bonus Score
Profitability by Vehicle Age
Geographic Analysis
Volatility Assessment
Statistical Validation
Key Findings
Recommendations


This presentation analyzes a Spanish motor insurance portfolio using visual analytics techniques. We examine univariate distributions of key variables and bivariate relationships to identify factors influencing profitability and volatility. The analysis employs ggplot2 and statistical testing to validate findings and provide actionable insights for portfolio management.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Introduction & Objectives
Dataset: Spanish motor insurance portfolio
Key Questions:

What are the distribution patterns of premiums and claims?
Which factors are associated with portfolio profitability?
What patterns are observed in claims volatility across segments?

Methods: Univariate and bivariate visual analytics using ggplot2 and ggstatsplot

The study analyzes real-world insurance data to understand profitability drivers and risk patterns. We use tidyverse for data preparation and ggplot2 extensions for visualization. The objective is to identify actionable insights for pricing strategy and risk management through statistical validation of observed patterns.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Data Overview
Dataset Size: 352,338 policies (from 354,140 original records)
Key Variables: Premium, Claims, Vehicle Type, Geographic Area, Vehicle Age
Derived Metrics: Profitability, Volatility (CV)

The dataset contains over 600,000 insurance policies with information on premiums, claims, vehicle characteristics, and geographic location. We engineered profitability as the difference between premium and claim cost. Missing values were minimal and removed. This comprehensive dataset enables robust statistical analysis of factors affecting insurance portfolio performance.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Normality Assessment


Q-Q plots show clear deviations from normality for all variables. Premium and claims are right-skewed; profitability shows left-tail deviation. Non-parametric tests (Kruskal-Wallis, Mann-Whitney) are appropriate for group comparisons.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Premium Distribution


Premium distribution is strongly right-skewed with most policies concentrated between €300-€600. The long tail indicates a small proportion of high-premium policies. This pattern is typical for insurance portfolios where standard coverage dominates. The skewness suggests potential for segmentation strategies targeting different premium tiers with tailored products and pricing approaches.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Claims Distribution


Claims distribution shows extreme right-skewness with high concentration at zero, indicating most policies have no claims. Among policies with claims, costs vary widely with a long tail of expensive claims. This pattern reflects the fundamental insurance principle where few policyholders generate claims. The high zero-inflation suggests opportunities for no-claims bonus programs to retain low-risk customers.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Profitability Patterns


Overall profitability distribution centers around positive values, indicating a healthy portfolio. However, substantial variance exists with a significant proportion of unprofitable policies. The distribution is slightly right-skewed, suggesting more extreme profits than losses. This pattern validates the portfolio’s viability while highlighting the need to identify and mitigate loss-making segments through improved risk assessment and pricing adjustments.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Premium vs Claims Relationship


The scatter plot reveals a weak positive correlation between premiums and claim costs (Spearman ρ ≈ 0.1-0.2). This suggests current pricing may not fully capture underlying risk factors. A stronger correlation would indicate better risk-based pricing. The weak relationship highlights opportunities to refine pricing models by incorporating additional risk factors such as vehicle type, driver characteristics, and geographic location to better align premiums with expected claims.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Profitability by Fuel Type (Detailed)


Boxplot shows the distribution of profitability across fuel types with extreme outliers filtered for clarity. The median profitability is similar across fuel types, with slight variations. The dashed red line at zero helps identify unprofitable policies.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Geographic Analysis


Geographic location is significantly associated with profitability, with substantial variation across areas. Some regions consistently generate higher profits while others underperform. Statistical tests confirm these differences are significant (p < 0.001). Regional variations likely reflect differences in claim frequency, repair costs, fraud rates, and driving conditions. This finding supports geographic risk-based pricing and targeted market strategies for high-performing regions.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Profitability by Region (Statistical Test)


Statistical validation shows area U has slightly higher median profitability than area R. The difference is statistically significant but the effect size is very small, indicating circulation area alone doesn’t strongly explain profitability differences.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Raincloud Plot: Profitability by Area


Raincloud plots combine density curves, boxplots, and individual data points to show complete distributional information. This advanced visualization reveals not just central tendency but also spread, skewness, and outliers across geographic areas, enabling more nuanced risk assessment.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Profitability by Vehicle Age


Profitability varies across vehicle age groups. Older vehicles, especially 41-50 and 50+ groups, show wider negative tails, suggesting higher downside risk. Most policies across all age groups have profitability close to zero or moderately positive.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Profitability by Policy Type


Policy type shows the most meaningful group difference in the analysis. COMP_N and COMP_E (comprehensive policies) have the highest median profitability, while TP (basic third-party) has the lowest. This is likely because comprehensive policies generate higher premiums that offset claim costs. Although the effect size is still relatively small, this is the most actionable finding for pricing strategy.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Total Exposure vs Profitability


Total exposure has the strongest correlation with profitability (Spearman ρ = 0.565) — the highest of any variable in the analysis. Longer-active policies earn more premium, which directly increases profitability. However, this relationship is partly structural: profitability is constructed from premium, which scales with exposure. Future analysis should use exposure-adjusted metrics for fairer comparisons.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Profitability by Bonus Score


Bonus score shows a counterintuitive pattern: the B group (poor claims history) has the highest median profitability (~€333), while G (good claims history) has the lowest (~€229). This may reflect that B-group policyholders pay higher premiums that more than offset their claims. However, the B and N groups are much smaller than G, so results should be interpreted cautiously. The effect size is small.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Volatility Assessment


Claims volatility, measured by coefficient of variation, differs substantially across fuel types. Higher volatility indicates greater uncertainty in claim costs, representing increased risk exposure. Certain segments show particularly high volatility, requiring larger reserves and potentially higher risk premiums. Understanding volatility patterns enables better capital allocation and risk management strategies to ensure portfolio stability and solvency.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Statistical Validation


Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis tests confirm that profitability differences across vehicle types are statistically significant (p < 0.001). The effect size indicates practical significance beyond statistical significance. Pairwise comparisons reveal which specific vehicle types differ significantly from each other. This rigorous statistical validation ensures our findings are not due to random chance and provides confidence for implementing data-driven pricing and underwriting decisions.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Key Findings
Univariate:

Premium right-skewed (most policies < €500); claims 88.1% zero-inflated; profitability has long negative left tail
Portfolio dominated by diesel (63.9%), CC policy type (57.9%), good claims history (bonus G)

Bivariate:

Total exposure strongest predictor of profitability (Spearman ρ = 0.565) — longer policies earn more premium
Policy type most meaningful group difference: COMP_N/COMP_E more profitable than TP
All other categorical factors (fuel, area, vehicle age, driver age, experience, municipality, bonus score) are statistically significant but with very small effect sizes
Premium vs claims: weak relationship (ρ = 0.217) — premium alone does not predict claim cost

Volatility:

Gasoline vehicles and Area R show highest claim cost volatility (CV)
All CV measures inflated by zero-inflation; treat as relative risk indicators


Key takeaway: no single factor strongly explains profitability. Policy type and total exposure are the most actionable variables. Statistical significance across many variables is largely driven by the large sample size rather than strong practical effects.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Presentation): Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
text:
Recommendations
1. Policy Type Pricing

Comprehensive policies (COMP_N, COMP_E) are more profitable — review whether TP premiums adequately cover liability exposure

2. Claim Severity Management

A small number of extreme claims drive most losses — consider differentiated coverage terms or reinsurance for high-severity segments

3. Vehicle Age Risk

Older vehicles (41–50 years, 50+) show wider negative profitability tails — tighten underwriting criteria for very old vehicles

4. Geographic Volatility

Area R has higher claim volatility than Area U — adjust pricing and reserving assumptions by region

5. Exposure-Adjusted Analysis

Total exposure strongly correlates with profitability by construction — use exposure-adjusted metrics for fairer segment comparisons in future analysis


Recommendations are grounded in the actual effect sizes observed. Policy type and claim severity management are the highest-priority actions. Geographic and vehicle age adjustments are secondary but statistically supported.
